title: SEGMENT 9 — Genome-scale engineering: MAGE, REXER, and the multi-locus editing era

lines.text: SEGMENT 9 — Genome-scale engineering: MAGE, REXER, and the multi-locus editing era
I want to close the first half of the lecture with the modern tools that have changed how fast metabolic engineering can iterate.

Because for a long time — for the first three decades of the field — you would make one or two genome edits per round and then characterize the strain, and a complete project would involve a few dozen edits over several years.
The bottleneck was the rate at which you could make and combine edits.
In the last fifteen years, that bottleneck has been broken open by a set of tools collectively called multi-locus or genome-scale editing.
The breakthrough tool, the one that opened the door, was MAGE — Multiplex Automated Genome Engineering, developed in George Church's lab at Harvard around 2009, with Harris Wang as lead author.
MAGE built on a technique called recombineering — recombination-mediated genetic engineering — which had been developed in the early 2000s.

We have a whole separate set of material on recombineering, so I will keep this short, but the gist is that you can use a viral protein called lambda Beta to drive the integration of single-stranded DNA oligonucleotides into the bacterial chromosome at any homologous site.
Feed a cell a short oligo with the edit you want, expose it to lambda Beta, and the oligo gets incorporated into the chromosome at the corresponding location during DNA replication.
One oligo, one edit.
MAGE took this and multiplied it.
Instead of feeding the cell one oligo, you feed it a pool of dozens of oligos targeting different sites simultaneously.
Some cells take up one oligo, some take up several.
After a round, you have a population of cells with various combinations of the edits.
You grow them out, repeat the cycle — feed in more oligos, integrate more edits — and after many cycles, some cells will have accumulated many of the edits at once.

The Church lab demonstrated this by simultaneously editing dozens of sites involved in lycopene production, an industrially relevant terpenoid pigment, and dramatically boosting lycopene yields in a handful of rounds.
The whole thing could be automated on a benchtop robot — hence "Automated" in the name — running thousands of edits per day across millions of cells.
MAGE was a watershed.
It demonstrated that you did not have to make edits one at a time.
You could make many at once, generate combinatorial libraries of edits, and find the winning combinations by selection or screening.
The pace of strain engineering accelerated by an order of magnitude.
The cost per edit collapsed.

Building on that, Jason Chin's lab at the MRC LMB developed a related but distinct technology called REXER — Replicon EXcision for Enhanced Recombination — and its sister technology GENESIS, for synthesizing large stretches of recoded genome and inserting them into E. coli chromosomes.
REXER lets you replace long stretches of chromosome — tens of kilobases at a time — with synthetic DNA carrying many designed changes.

Chin's group used these tools to build E. coli with completely recoded genomes — Syn61, for example, an E. coli in which every instance of one of the redundant codons was swapped out across the entire genome — a genome with thousands of synonymous changes built up over many rounds of REXER.
That kind of multi-locus, multi-kilobase editing was, twenty years ago, simply inconceivable.
Today it is routine in the leading labs.
There is also, sitting alongside these recombineering-based tools, the CRISPR-based editing era.
We will come back to CRISPR for metabolic engineering in Segment 16 specifically.

The headline is that CRISPR lets you make precise edits at any specified site with high efficiency, knock out genes, suppress genes with CRISPRi, activate them with CRISPRa, and combine many targeted manipulations more easily than ever before.

CRISPR is, for metabolic engineering, less a revolutionary new capability than a substantial cost reduction in capabilities that recombineering had already pioneered, but the cost reduction has been transformative for the throughput of engineering campaigns.

The analogy I want you to carry: MAGE and the multi-locus editing tools are the discipline's equivalent of the move from hand-soldering electronic components onto a breadboard to mass-fabricating silicon wafers with millions of transistors at once.
Pre-MAGE, you made one or two edits at a time.
Post-MAGE, you can make dozens at once and combine them combinatorially.
The throughput of strain engineering campaigns has increased by orders of magnitude.
That capacity is what lets a modern company like Ginkgo Bioworks — which we will meet in Segment 11 — run dozens of strain engineering projects in parallel across many target molecules.
The relationship to recombineering is direct.

If you want to dig deeper into how MAGE actually works — how lambda Beta finds its targets, how oligo design affects integration efficiency, how you guard against off-target effects — those mechanistic details belong in the recombineering material.
The metabolic engineering perspective here is just: this is the tool that lets you make many genome edits cheaply and in parallel, and the modern field would not exist in its current form without it.
That is the end of the first hour.
Let us take a fifteen-minute break.

When we come back, we will start moving through the commercial waves — Amyris and biofuels, Ginkgo Bioworks and the strain factory model, the plant-derived natural products, sustainable manufacturing, alt-protein, modular cloning, CRISPR, AI-driven design, and finally the frontier work on carbon-neutral biomanufacturing.
The second half is where the rubber meets the road, where you will see what this discipline has actually produced and where it is going.
See you in fifteen.
